title-group.article-title: Cisplatin Eligibility and Outcomes in Metastatic Urothelial Carcinoma: A Prospective Cohort Study

abstract.p: Background: Many patients with metastatic urothelial carcinoma of the bladder are ineligible for cisplatin-based chemotherapy. We characterised the proportion of patients who are cisplatin-ineligible and compared outcomes between cisplatin-eligible and cisplatin-ineligible groups receiving gemcitabine plus carboplatin.

Methods: Prospective cohort study of 680 patients with metastatic urothelial carcinoma from 12 academic centres. Cisplatin eligibility was defined using standardised criteria including creatinine clearance, hearing, and performance status.

Results: Forty-two per cent of patients were cisplatin-ineligible. Cisplatin-eligible patients receiving gemcitabine-cisplatin had significantly better overall survival than cisplatin-ineligible patients receiving gemcitabine-carboplatin (median 16.3 vs 11.4 months).

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Urothelial carcinoma of the bladder is frequently diagnosed at an advanced stage in elderly patients with significant comorbidities. Cisplatin-based combination chemotherapy (gemcitabine plus cisplatin) is the preferred first-line regimen for metastatic disease in patients with adequate renal function and performance status. However, a substantial proportion of patients with bladder cancer are ineligible for cisplatin due to renal insufficiency, hearing loss, peripheral neuropathy, or poor performance status.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This prospective observational cohort enrolled consecutive patients with locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma commencing first-line chemotherapy between January 2015 and December 2018. Cisplatin eligibility was assessed using the Galsky criteria: creatinine clearance greater than or equal to 60 mL/min, ECOG performance status 0-1, adequate hearing (grade 1 or less CTCAE), and peripheral neuropathy grade 1 or less. Patients were followed until death or last contact.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Of 680 evaluable patients, 286 (42%) were cisplatin-ineligible. The most common reasons for ineligibility were impaired renal function (58%), poor performance status (31%), and hearing loss (22%). Among cisplatin-eligible patients receiving gemcitabine-cisplatin, median overall survival was 16.3 months (95% CI 14.8-17.9) versus 11.4 months (95% CI 10.1-12.8) for cisplatin-ineligible patients receiving gemcitabine-carboplatin (HR 1.58; 95% CI 1.27-1.97).

Objective response rates were 58% for cisplatin-eligible and 42% for cisplatin-ineligible patients. Grade 3 or higher haematological toxicity was more frequent with cisplatin-based therapy (42% vs 38%) but non-haematological toxicity profiles were similar.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Cisplatin ineligibility affects a substantial proportion of patients with metastatic bladder cancer and is associated with inferior outcomes. Development of effective carboplatin-substituted and non-platinum regimens for cisplatin-ineligible patients remains a priority.
